intention: Train LoRA v2 on curated dataset
strategy: 59 images (47 ComfyUI + 11 Leonardo + 1 v20_04 ref). Per-image textfile captions. 10 epochs, lr 1e-4, lora_rank 16, lora_alpha 16, cosine schedule. SimpleTuner on Mac Mini.
expectation: Expected: LoRA learns Flo's face as a transferable concept. Should produce Flo in any scene when triggered with flo_character.
result_notes: Training completed (590 steps, ~1hr). Produced consistent face but NOT Flo's face. LoRA converged on averaged mean of all training images. Learned scene (golden hour + denim jacket) fused with face concept.
lesson_learned: Homogeneous training data is fatal. All 59 images had same lighting, same framing, same expression, nearly identical captions. LoRA couldn't isolate face from scene. Mixed rendering engines (ComfyUI + Leonardo) averaged into neither. The face consistency problem we're trying to solve with the LoRA was INSIDE the training data itself.
fix_for_next_time: v3 needs: (1) diverse angles/lighting/expressions/backgrounds so face is the ONLY constant, (2) single rendering engine, (3) fewer epochs to avoid overtraining, (4) regularization images (non-Flo faces) so model learns what Flo is NOT

intention: Generate 3D model of Flo from single reference image (v20_04)
strategy: Single image-to-3D with Meshy-6, PBR enabled
result_notes: 95% there. Eyes/hair/outfit good. Neck too long, mouth crease wrong, teeth and lips funky.
lesson_learned: Single front-facing image produces a good starting point but Meshy guesses poorly on geometry it cant see (teeth, profile).
fix_for_next_time: Use multi-image with front + 3/4 + profile views for better reconstruction.

intention: Generate 3D model of Flo from 4 reference images (front + 3/4 + 2 profiles)
strategy: Multi-image-to-3D with Meshy-6, PBR enabled, 4 source views
expectation: Improved face geometry from multiple angles, especially mouth and teeth
result_notes: Eyes and mouth crease improved over v1. Teeth and lips still off but better overall. Erik noted improvements.
lesson_learned: Multi-image gives better results than single. More angles help but teeth/lips still struggle — may need higher polycount or retexture.
fix_for_next_time: Try higher target_polycount (60000) to give face geometry more budget. Consider retexture if mesh is close.

intention: Double polycount from default 30k to 60k to improve face geometry (teeth, lips)
strategy: Multi-image-to-3D with Meshy-6, target_polycount 60000, same 4 source images as v2
expectation: More geometric detail in face — better teeth, lips, mouth definition
result_notes: Face flatter than v2. Eyes less defined. Smile more forced/wide. Skin tone washed out. Denim jacket got more detail but face went backward. More polys went to clothing, not face.
lesson_learned: Doubling polycount from 30k to 60k made clothing better but face worse. Meshy distributes extra polys to visually complex areas (jacket stitching) not the face.

intention: Test different source image — white tee instead of denim jacket to reduce clothing complexity and let Meshy focus on face
strategy: Single image-to-3D with Meshy-6, default 30k polycount, simple clothing source image
expectation: Better face geometry without jacket distracting the reconstruction. Only variable changed: source image.
result_notes: Completely different person. Source image (flo_train_261) is from batch grinder with different seed — not the same Flo as v20_04. Ranked worst of all 4 versions. Erik: 'somebody completely different.'
lesson_learned: Cannot swap source images between different Flo generations. Face identity is tied to the specific seed/render. All source images must come from v20_04 or renders derived from it.
fix_for_next_time: Only use v20_04 and its derived angle/profile renders as Meshy source images. To change clothing, generate new renders from ComfyUI with same seed but different outfit prompt.

intention: Redo v4 properly — only change one variable (front source image) from v3 settings
strategy: Multi-image-to-3D with Meshy-6, 60k polycount. Only change from v3: swapped front image from v20_04 to flo_train_261_white_tee. Same 3 angle/profile shots.
expectation: Isolate source image as variable. If face is still different, confirms training images are a different Flo identity.

intention: Step up polycount from 60k to 120k — same source images and settings as v3
strategy: Multi-image-to-3D with Meshy-6, 120k polycount. Only change from v3: target_polycount 60000 -> 120000.
expectation: More face geometry detail. Compare directly to v3 (60k) to isolate polycount effect.
result_notes: Teeth better than v3. Eyes look funny. Face better overall. Chin more pointed. Space between eyes much wider than source.
lesson_learned: 120k polycount improves teeth geometry but introduces face proportion changes (wider eye spacing, pointed chin).

intention: Continue polycount sweep — 240k, same source images and settings as v3/v6
strategy: Multi-image-to-3D with Meshy-6, 240k polycount. Only change from v6: target_polycount 120000 -> 240000.
expectation: Compare to v6 (120k) to see if more polys continues improving face or hits diminishing returns.
result_notes: Much better than v6. Best mesh render so far by Erik's assessment. Doesn't look exactly like Flo but best AI mesh quality achieved. Best of the polycount sweep.
lesson_learned: 240k is the sweet spot so far in the polycount sweep (30k -> 60k -> 120k -> 240k). Higher polycount doesn't linearly increase triangle count but does improve face quality.

intention: Fix harsh lighting from yesterday's first render — too hot key and rim lights
strategy: Rebalanced 3-point lighting: key 200->120W size 2->4, fill 80->40W size 3->5, rim 150->60W size 2->3.5. AgX Base Contrast, exposure 0.3, gamma 1.05. Dark warm background.
expectation: Softer shadows, no blown highlights, more natural skin tones.
result_notes: Dramatic improvement. Soft shadows, natural skin tones, warm feel. Erik confirmed 'much better'. Teeth issue remains (texture problem, not lighting).
lesson_learned: Larger light size = softer shadows. Key:Fill:Rim ratio of 3:1:1.5 works well for character portraits.
fix_for_next_time: Start with these settings as baseline for all future renders.

intention: Retexture v7 with face-emphasis prompt to fix teeth and improve face detail
strategy: meshy_retexture on v7_240k task. Prompt: detailed face, clear eyes, natural skin, clean teeth. PBR enabled, remove_lighting true.
expectation: Better teeth and eye detail while keeping v7 geometry.
result_notes: Made everything worse. Lost freckles, braces-like teeth, spaghetti hair, changed hair color to blonde, cross-eyed, changed outfit entirely. Lost character identity.
lesson_learned: Meshy retexture with generic face prompt destroys character identity. The original texture with its flaws is better than a retexture that loses the character.
fix_for_next_time: If retexturing, must explicitly preserve: auburn hair, freckles, warm skin, specific outfit. Or skip retexture entirely.

settings.text_style_prompt: High quality character portrait. Clear bright eyes with detailed iris and pupils, natural healthy skin with subtle freckles, clean white teeth with defined individual teeth, soft natural lips, warm skin tone. Detailed face features, studio quality texture.

intention: First ControlNet test — use depth maps from Blender renders to guide ComfyUI generation
strategy: Blender compositor extracts inverted mist depth maps from 4 angles. Feed to ComfyUI with ControlNetApplyAdvanced (depth-sdxl, strength 0.65). RealCartoon Pony V3. Original FLO_FACE prompt with denim jacket.
expectation: Depth map locks pose/composition while prompt and checkpoint handle style. Should look like Flo in the 3D model's pose.
result_notes: PIPELINE WORKS! Front render was amazing — Erik said 'omg!!! how cute!!!!' But inconsistent across angles: front was more tan with tight curls, profile went leather jacket with ringlets, 3/4 had yellow collar. Different seed per angle caused style drift.
lesson_learned: ControlNet depth at 0.65 locks pose beautifully but doesn't constrain style details (hair texture, skin tone, clothing). Each angle with a different seed produces a different interpretation.
fix_for_next_time: Lock seed across angles. Tighten prompt for skin tone, hair texture, clothing.

intention: Test if locking seed across all angles improves consistency
strategy: Same as v1 but all 4 angles use seed 1775764319 (from the front 'omg' render).
expectation: Same style DNA across all angles — consistent skin tone, hair texture, clothing.
result_notes: Skin tone consistency improved — all 4 are clearly the same person. But profile still went leather jacket with tight ringlets. Low angle was best — denim vest, loose curls. Seed helps but doesn't fully solve clothing/hair drift.
lesson_learned: Locked seed improves consistency but the depth map changes composition enough that the seed can't maintain every detail. Need tighter prompt too.
fix_for_next_time: Combine locked seed with tighter prompt and aggressive negative.

intention: Locked seed + tighter prompt + t-shirt to maximize consistency
strategy: Seed 1775764319 + revised prompt: 'light warm peach skin' (not tanned), 'loose wavy curly hair shoulder length' (not tight curls), 'simple white t-shirt' (not denim jacket). Negative adds: leather jacket, tight curls, ringlets, dark skin, jacket, coat, hoodie.
expectation: All 4 angles should show same person, same skin tone, same hair texture, no jacket.
result_notes: WINNER. All 4 angles are clearly the same person. Consistent light warm skin, loose waves, freckles, white top. No more leather jacket. No more ringlets. Profile went from worst to beautiful. Erik: 'Holy crap, I think we have a winner!'
lesson_learned: The winning formula: locked seed + tight prompt (specific skin/hair/clothing terms) + aggressive negative (explicitly ban unwanted items). ControlNet depth 0.65 for pose lock. This is the recipe for training data generation.
fix_for_next_time: Use this exact configuration as the template for generating training data batches.
